created_by: Wilderculture WILD Toolbox session (Claude) — scrub group batch; field sheet 09 split per type. v3.3.

ukhab_identity.composite_pk_note: Keyed at h3j (Willow scrub) — the willow-dominated lowland-to-midslope and wet scrub. Distinct from h1c6 montane willow scrub (the Annex I sub-arctic treeline willows) and from h3h mixed scrub.
ukhab_identity.secondary_codes_notes: Grades to wet woodland (w1d) at the wet end as willows mature into carr.
ukhab_identity.annex1_habitat: None at this (lowland/wet) willow-scrub level; the sub-arctic montane willow scrub is the Annex I type (h1c6, H4080).

sources.title: UK Habitat Classification v2.01 (master data sheet + BNG code list)
sources.specific_claim: h3j = Willow scrub (confirmed in the UKHab master data sheet and BNG code list: 'Willow scrub h3j').

nvc_communities: W1–W3 Salix cinerea / grey willow scrub and carr (the scrub end)
(grading into W4/W7 wet woodland as it matures)
cross_references.nvc_communities_notes: Willow scrub is the scrub/early-carr end of the wet-woodland series; matures into W4/W7 wet woodland (w1d).
cross_references.phase1_equivalent: A2.1 Dense scrub (willow); wet willow carr

sources.title: Natural England — scrub community and shrub species profiles
sources.specific_claim: Willow scrub communities and their value.

ecology.description: Willow-dominated scrub — grey, eared and goat willow, with rowan, birch, holly, bird cherry and aspen — the pioneer scrub that runs up the fell above the thorn-and-hazel ground, and the wet scrub of valley floors and flushes. The single most important part of the scrub family for two reasons: willow is the most nutritious, most palatable browse on the hill, and its early catkins are among the first and most important nectar and pollen sources of the upland spring. A pollinator engine and an invertebrate powerhouse.
condition_indicators_good: A multi-age, structurally diverse willow mosaic with regeneration
Mixed with rowan/birch and integrated with open ground/wet flush
Browsing light enough that regeneration survives
condition_indicators_poor: Absent — grazed off the hill (the usual state)
Geriatric: old willows with no young coming through
Drained/dried where it should be wet willow
ecosystem_functions: The best browse on the hill (willow shoots high in nutrition and palatability)
A critical and under-appreciated EARLY pollinator resource — willow catkins sustain the bilberry bumblebee and other early bees
An invertebrate powerhouse — willow alone supports a vast community (leaf beetles, moths, host-specific galling sawflies incl. a Scottish endemic)
Soil-builder; water retention and the route to wet woodland on wet ground
ecology.whole_system_role: The pioneer and pollinator engine of the scrub mosaic, and the wet-ground bridge from open habitats to wet woodland (w1d).

successional_neighbours.habitat_code: g1b acid grassland / f2b rush pasture / h1b6 wet heath (open ground)
successional_neighbours.note: Pioneers onto released open and wet ground.
On wet ground willow scrub matures into willow/alder/birch carr (wet woodland).
The high sub-arctic willows (downy/woolly/whortle-leaved) are the separate Annex I montane type.

sources.title: Wilderculture field sheet 09 — Upland Scrub
sources.specific_claim: Willow as the best browse and the early-catkin pollinator engine; the willow invertebrate community.

ukhab_condition.condition_grade_note: Assigned per parcel; regeneration is the diagnostic.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Favourable
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Multi-age willow mosaic with regeneration and abundant catkins; integrated with open/wet ground.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Unfavourable declining
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Geriatric (no young), drying where it should be wet, or rank thicket.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Absent
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Grazed off — no willow where it should occur.

abiotic_template.parent_material_geology: Spans the gradient; often wetter ground and flushes
abiotic_template.altitude_range_m: Valley floor up the fell toward the treeline (below the montane willows)
abiotic_template.aspect_exposure: Pioneers on a range of ground; willow/carr at the wet end
abiotic_template.bioclimate_notes: Throughout the uplands; wet willow on valley floors and flushes.

elemental_conditions.convention_note: CURRENT-STATE reading. Bioregionally Wilder; new willow on released ground = Regenerate→Wilder.

water.descriptor: Pioneer willow on a range of ground; wet willow on flushes/valley floors (grades to wet woodland).

air.descriptor: Aerobic on dry ground; fluctuating in wet willow.

fire.descriptor: High production and high-quality energy — the best browse and the key early pollinator resource; energy flows strongly up the web.

range_of_natural_variability: Wet willow carr maturing to wet woodland
successional_envelope.successional_pathway: open/wet ground → pioneer willow scrub → (wet) willow scrub → wet woodland (w1d) / mixed woodland. A fast colonist where browsing drops.

wild_assessment.energy_flow_role: The pollinator engine and best browse of the scrub family; the wet-ground bridge to wet woodland.
wild_assessment.wild_zone_rationale: Bioregionally Wilder — willow is the fastest, richest part of the scrub return; new willow across released open and wet ground is the core Regenerate→Wilder action. Established wet willow carr links into Protect & Restore wet woodland (w1d).
wild_assessment.disturbance_regime: Herbivore-pressure managed. Reduce deer/sheep and willow returns fast where a seed source survives (willow is among the readiest colonists).

wild_management_target.detail: Let willow colonise and form a multi-age mosaic by reducing browsing; protect the early-catkin pollinator resource; allow wet willow to mature to carr/wet woodland on wet ground. Protect remnants as seed sources.
integrity_indicators: bilberry bumblebee and the willow invertebrate community
maturation to wet woodland on wet ground

wild_assessment.ecological_value_notes: High — the pollinator powerhouse and invertebrate richest part of the scrub family, and the best browse on the hill. Strategic value as the fastest scrub colonist of degraded/wet ground.

rarity_irreplaceability.notes: Willow returns readily where pressure drops; not rare in potential, though currently scarce on the overgrazed hill.

diversity.notes: Willow supports a vast invertebrate community and is a critical early pollinator resource.

energy_flow_contribution.notes: Best browse and key early nectar/pollen; high-quality energy up the web.

naturalistic_lu_ha.value: No single figure — reduce herbivore pressure for regeneration; not a stocking rate
naturalistic_lu_ha.basis_note: As for all scrub: manage herbivore pressure, not a stocking number. Willow is among the fastest to return when browsing drops.

wild_assessment.mosaic_role_note: The fastest pioneer and the pollinator engine of the mosaic; nurses other regeneration and bridges to wet woodland.
wild_assessment.preferential_grazing_magnet_note: TRUE and the strongest case — willow is the best browse on the hill, eaten first and recovering last; its quality is exactly why it is absent.
wild_assessment.process_indicators_note: Pollinator resource and regeneration are the key measures.

forage.overall_palatability_notes: The single most palatable, most nutritious browse on the hill — willow shoots above all. That quality is its undoing: eaten first, recovering last. NOT a forage resource to stock; the aim is regeneration via REMOVING pressure.

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: Not a forage/stocking habitat — high browse value is the regeneration constraint. Managed by reducing herbivore pressure.

conservation_lu_ha.value: No single figure — reduce herbivore pressure until willow regenerates
conservation_lu_ha.basis_note: No single grazing figure; willow returns fast when browsing drops.

sources.title: Wilderculture field sheet 09 — Upland Scrub
sources.specific_claim: No single grazing figure; reduce pressure for regeneration.

forage.palatability_rank: Very High (best browse on the hill) — a regeneration liability
forage.nutritional_quality_note: Highly nutritious browse; soil-builder. No toxicity theme.

seasonal_forage_curve.spring: Catkins (key pollinator resource) and fresh high-value browse — eaten first

optimal_grazing_animals.notes: Willow is the readiest colonist when pressure drops; light fluctuating disturbance can maintain an established mosaic.

unsuitable_animals.reason: Strip the best browse before it can establish — the reason willow is absent.

timing_windows.rationale: Willow returns fast when pressure drops

management.intensity_guidance: Reduce herbivore pressure (no stocking figure). Protect catkin/pollinator resource; let wet willow mature to carr/wet woodland.
management.regulatory_note: Do not burn or drain wet willow. Where willow scrub borders watercourses, note natural flood-storage value. Check designated-site consents.
management.monitoring_protocol: Willow regeneration/advance, catkin abundance, willow invertebrate community, wet character, maturation to wet woodland. Apply the re-grade trigger.

bng_unit_value.value_note: Medium distinctiveness as willow scrub; high functional/pollinator value.

sources.title: Statutory Biodiversity Metric
sources.specific_claim: Willow scrub distinctiveness; confirm exact label.

key_positive_indicators: willow regeneration and a multi-age mosaic
integrity_indicators_fauna: bilberry bumblebee Bombus monticola and other early bees on the catkins (the pollinator-engine signal)
the vast willow invertebrate community — leaf beetles, moths, host-specific galling sawflies incl. a Scottish endemic
willow warbler, lesser redpoll, whinchat; black grouse using the mosaic
target_species_composition: wet willow maturing to carr where wet
species_list.note: Skeleton only — full records in Phase 2B. The willow→pollinator and willow→invertebrate relationships are the key integrity links.

fields_fully_populated: the willow browse/pollinator/invertebrate value; herbivore-pressure management
the link to wet woodland (w1d) and the distinction from montane willow (h1c6)
fields_partially_populated: wet vs dry willow distinction — per parcel
fields_pending_research: Willow invertebrate records incl. the Scottish endemic sawfly
data_completeness.notes: Split from the scrub sheet because the sheet so strongly emphasises willow's distinct role — the best browse and the key EARLY pollinator resource (catkins → bilberry bumblebee), plus a vast invertebrate community. Bioregionally Wilder; the fastest scrub colonist and the wet-ground bridge to wet woodland (w1d). Distinct from the Annex I montane willow scrub (h1c6).
